text: While there is a plethora of literature concerning the historical development of Pentecostalism in Ghana, relatively little has been written concerning the pneumatological dimensions of the missional theology of the movement. Utilising a trinitarian understanding as a departure point this article examines the missional role of the Holy Spirit within Ghanaian Pentecostal understandings and practices. It engages official documents, liturgy and oral testimony from Ghanaian Pentecostal church leaders in order to understand how the “Spirit phenomenon” such as baptism and being filled with the Holy Spirit functions in empowering believers in order to conduct mission. A Ghanaian Pentecostal understanding affirms that the Holy Spirit as the agent of the *missio Dei* is missionally operative in two ways: convicting the world of its sinfulness and empowering believers with gifts in order that they may serve and witness in the world of humanity. Without the Spirit’s presence the Ghanaian Pentecumenical Pentecostal church members argue that any mission work is nothing more than humanistic endeavour. Thus, in a Ghanaian context, the practise of maintaining a Spirit-filled life through acts of prayer and Bible study amongst other things serve as missiological tools for the mission work undertaken by evangelists and other believers.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
